rt1-4-1.title: Hands-on: expose with a decaying Ir-192 source
rt1-4-1.intro: Your exposure device holds a 40 Ci Ir-192 source with a 73.8-day half-life - it is always on and always decaying. Run a gamma exposure to the film-density window, then prove you can compensate when the source has decayed for a month.

tasks.label: Expose the film to a density between 2.0 and 3.0
tasks.hint: Exposure E = activity x time - if the film reads light (low D), lengthen the exposure time.

tasks.label: Jump 30 days ahead and hit the density window again
tasks.hint: 30 days is about 0.4 half-life, so activity fell to roughly 75% - stretch the time about a third longer.

rt1-4-2.title: Hands-on: small focal spot, sharp radiograph
rt1-4-2.intro: The line-focus target of your directional tube spreads heat over a big area yet shows the beam only a 1.5 mm effective focal spot. Use that small spot well: set source-to-object distance and film gap so geometric unsharpness Ug = f x OFD / SOD stays in spec.

tasks.label: Bring Ug to 0.3 mm or less with the 1.5 mm spot
tasks.hint: Ug = f x OFD / SOD - back the tube head away or close the object-to-film gap.

tasks.label: Place the wire IQI on the source-side weld surface
tasks.hint: The IQI proves the sharpness you set up - it belongs on the face toward the tube, never the film side.

rt1-4-3.title: Hands-on: betatron sharpness on 25 mm steel
rt1-4-3.intro: A betatron is prized for sharpness because its focal spot is tiny - here just 0.5 mm. On this thick 25 mm plate, hold geometric unsharpness to a razor-thin 0.15 mm spec and feel why high-energy machines with small spots win on image definition.

tasks.label: Hold Ug at or under 0.15 mm on the thick plate
tasks.hint: With f only 0.5 mm a modest SOD already helps - shrink the film gap or back the source off a little.

tasks.label: Set the IQI on the source-side surface of the part
tasks.hint: Same rule at any energy: the wire IQI sits on the face looking at the betatron, not against the film.

rt1-4-4.title: Hands-on: live-beam safety at the fluoroscope
rt1-4-4.intro: A fluoroscopic beam is ON the entire time you watch the screen - never put your eye (or your body) in it unprotected. Work the time-distance-shielding bay to keep a 15-minute live viewing session under the dose limit, just like the lead glass and standoff of a real-time cell.

tasks.label: Double your distance and watch the rate drop 4x
tasks.hint: Inverse-square law: twice as far from a live beam means one quarter of the dose rate.

tasks.label: Stack shielding until the boundary reads under 2 mR/h
tasks.hint: Each half-value layer cuts the rate in half - it is the same job the lead-glass viewing window does.

tasks.label: Plan a 15-minute viewing session under 10 mrem
tasks.hint: Dose = rate x hours - add slabs or stand farther back until 15 minutes of screen time fits the limit.
